title: The Experiment That Broke Its Participants
raw_summary: In 1971, Stanford psychologist Philip Zimbardo conducted a simulated prison experiment that had to be terminated after six days when guards began psychologically abusing prisoners. The study raised profound questions about situational versus dispositional behavior.

core_facts.statement: The Stanford Prison Experiment began on August 14, 1971 in the basement of Jordan Hall.
24 male college students were randomly assigned roles as either guards or prisoners.
Within 36 hours, the first prisoner had a complete emotional breakdown and had to be released.
Guards invented increasingly degrading punishments not sanctioned by the researchers.
Zimbardo himself became so absorbed in his role as 'Prison Superintendent' that he initially resisted stopping the experiment.
The experiment was terminated on day six, a week earlier than planned.
Later reanalysis revealed that guards had been explicitly coached to be tough, undermining the 'spontaneous' situational thesis.
